attributes.stem: Medical students are often present during surgical procedures in teaching hospitals to learn clinical skills and anatomy. A student is invited to perform a vaginal examination on an anaesthetised patient during a gynaecological procedure.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate ethical approach regarding performing intimate examinations under anaesthesia by medical students?
options: Students may examine if it benefits their education
Specific informed consent must be obtained before anaesthesia
Students may examine if the supervising consultant gives permission
Students may examine if they are supervised by a qualified doctor
Consent can be obtained from the next of kin
attributes.explanation:
The correct approach is that specific informed consent must be obtained before anaesthesia. Patients have the right to know who will be examining them and must give explicit permission for student involvement in intimate examinations before being anaesthetised. As with all histories, examinations and procedures, express consent must always be obtained before proceeding unless in life threatening situations where this may cause unacceptable delay to conserve life.

The incorrect options are problematic because:

  • While medical education is important, it never overrides the fundamental right of patient autonomy and consent and an intimate examination should not occur purely for the purpose of student education and benefit.

  • Medical staff cannot provide consent on behalf of patients for intimate examinations; therefore, students may examine if the supervising consultant gives permission is incorrect.

  • Students may examine if they are supervised by a qualified doctor is not correct, as supervision does not remove the requirement for explicit patient consent.

  • For an adult with capacity, only they can provide consent for intimate examinations; it would be inappropriate to obtain consent from the next of kin.

Performing intimate examinations without explicit prior consent is unethical and may constitute assault. Teaching hospitals should have clear policies regarding student involvement in intimate examinations under anaesthesia.
learningPoints: Intimate examinations under anaesthesia require explicit informed consent from the patient before the procedure.
Medical students must not perform intimate examinations on anaesthetised patients without prior consent.
Teaching hospitals must have clear policies regarding student involvement in intimate examinations under anaesthesia.
references: Kaur K, Salwi S, McNew K, et al. Medical Student Perspectives on the Ethics of Pelvic Exams Under Anesthesia: A Multi-Institutional Study✰. J Surg Educ. 2022;79(6):1413-1421. doi:10.1016/j.jsurg.2022.05.015

Cundall HL, MacPhedran SE, Arora KS. Consent for Pelvic Examinations Under Anesthesia by Medical Students: Historical Arguments and Steps Forward. Obstet Gynecol. 2019 Dec;134(6):1298-1302. doi: 10.1097/AOG.0000000000003509. PMID: 31764742; PMCID: PMC6905127.
Mandal J, Ponnambath DK, Parija SC. Bioethics: A brief review. Trop Parasitol. 2017;7(1):5-7. doi:10.4103/tp.TP_4_17
Varkey B. Principles of Clinical Ethics and Their Application to Practice. Med Princ Pract. 2021;30(1):17-28. doi:10.1159/000509119
